speaker: interviewer
text: [BEEPING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the 2nd of February, 1995. The survivor's name is Ellen Picker, P-I-C-K-E-R. Maiden name P-R-O-S-K-A-U-E-R The interview--

speaker: interviewer
text: '96, Sorry. The date is the 2nd of February, 1996. I'm sorry. The interviewer is Sharon Savdie. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is the 2nd of February, 1996. My name is Sharon Savdie, and I'm going to be conducting an interview with Ellen Picker, maiden name Proskauer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Proskauer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Proskauer. The interview will be conducted in English. And we are in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ellen Picker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-double L-E-N, P-I-C-K-E-R

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Proskauer, P-R-O-S-K-A-U-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your place of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was that prior to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany. Did you grow up in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 12 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that area and where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually, my-- my-- my mother-- we lived in my father's bachelor unit, which was very comfortable and very beautiful, all-- all my-- since-- till we went away. In a very nice district. And it was a lovely childhood. I had a wonderful childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were good. I mean, we had-- we had a servant, like everybody. Lower-- lower-- lower lower middle class had servants, too, you know, at the time. And of course, I was brought up more by the servants than by my mother. I can't remember that she went to park with me or something. I can't remember that. But it was a happy childhood. I had two cousins nearby, and I played there very often. I brought my bike.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell Me about your parents. Describe them for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother was a textile artist, and every-- always at a studio and always was very successful. I-- I-- I took a few photos. I'll show you what she-- she did things that they are not usually done. And with this she started in-- in-- in Poland and was very successful with-- with industrialists. And money was no obstacle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father was an apothecary. He had an apothecary. No? What is-- Like a pharmacy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pharmacy, yeah. And we lived a very comfortable life. No?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were an only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only child, yes. My father had a great sense of humor, and there was always laughs. And he was funny. But he was very strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I had-- if I said to my mother, no, that's not true. He would say, Ellen, he said, your mother is always right, even if she's not right, you know? He was old fashioned. He has to-- had to see what I was reading, and I had to be very punctual with him-- by the second. And I believe it was a mixed marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you describe that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I didn't see anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I didn't see-- both-- both didn't-- didn't practice their religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was a Protestant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Protestant, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did their families feel about the marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, I didn't know my-- my grandparents from my father's side. They died very early on. And he had a brother in Germany. And my cousin, actually, is-- lives in Australia, in Sydney. It's very fortunate. And there were only two brothers. And my uncle died in the concentration camp with one daughter, and the one daughter is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family celebrate Christmas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they celebrate any Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Christmas was always celebrated somewhere, you know, with presents. But I wasn't-- I wasn't told to-- to do religious things or anything like that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you aware of the fact that your father-- were you aware of the fact that your parents were from different cultural backgrounds?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that it was a mixed marriage, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was a-- he studied the language inside out. He was a poet, too. He wrote wonderful poetry. I just sent a whole lot of his poets to Germany, and my-- and my cousin will prepare it for me for-- for book binding. And I just felt I have to do that, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did beautiful poet-- especially in this loneliness, you know? He really suffered a loss with-- he loved my mother-- you can't imagine. It was a great love.

speaker: interviewee
text: 16 Years older, he was. He would be now 106-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 116, he would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: 115, he would be now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know very much about the-- the marriage and how it came about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I know my mother was engaged to somebody else. And when she met my father, she-- that was it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] He was a special man, a special man. He write beautiful poetry, and he-- he was a wonderful mathematician. That was his hobby. And he was so interesting about everything, you know? He-- I was much too young to-- to-- to appreciate it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your family perceived in the community? Were you seen as--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was always a very honored man everywhere. They loved him everywhere. When we-- when we were in Czechoslovakia, I remember I went once-- there was a very small town, Cieszyn, it was. And we went-- at night, you went back and forth, you know? A promenade. And all the people would always greet him and greet him. He was-- he always helped. He always gave good advice, and he was a wonderful man. And very humble. Very humble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know much about your father's family background?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but my-- my cousin always has a-- a family tree. And I don't know, I-- every few years, I get a new one. And the last time I got one, it was in sheets-- usually I got one that was rolled-- and I can't find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a matter of fact, I asked her, could I have a copy? She says, we'll do a new one now, because she has two new grandchildren and they'll make another family tree. Do you know if they had lived for many generations in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yes. Actually, this name is very-- it's a very rare name. The Proskauers were all sort of related. And I never met my grandparents, never. Only my uncle and my cousins. They lived in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father and mother, were they interested in music or politics?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very interested. She always had a music circle. She was-- as a child, she was material for-- for concerts they're giving, and so on. And later on, there was always a circle of-- they played for aunts, and so on. And she never gave that up. She still plays, but now she can't. She's-- she just-- she can't hear, and she's impossible. But she goes on. Chopin. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family circle of friends, what were they? Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish. I just thought of it. They were all Jewish. And we all wanted to migrate. And we'd-- we'd-- we had Spanish lessons. We bought books that-- there were books to learn languages. We did that ourselves in the circle. We wanted to go to South America. And friends went to South America, of my mother's. But they died, meanwhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- yes, and then the Czechoslovakian people came and fled from Czechoslovakia. And one night, my mother and I go-- go-- go on a walk on the street, and a lady comes and approaches us with two little boys. Where is the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]? What is that called? [NON-ENGLISH]? You know, where the Jewish offices are? What are-- how do you call it? Welfare.

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Welfare. Jewish welfare.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Where's, the Jewish welfare? It was closed. It was very nearby. And she's, ah. She said, I was hoping they would provide me for-- it was a woman and son-- we are-- we fled from Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took them along to our-- where we lived. We had also only a room or two. But my mother got her a room in the same establishment, and they were our guests.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother showed her the work she's doing. She was so stricken by that. She says, we have to come to Australia. And she helped us to come to Australia. They went to Adelaide. They were both doctors, the husband and wife. Yes. That was a lady who made us come to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was-- amongst your Jewish friends back in Germany, how was your parents' marriage perceived?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very-- all very free. I don't think anybody thought anything of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember that they had any other friends than Jewish friends. I just can't remember. There were-- it was an opera singer, and very many musicians, and my father had friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The house doctor. In the house we had very good friends. There was also a family with a mixed marriage. The-- the girl was five days older than I am, and we grew up together. And when Hitler came, the mother took-- the husband was Jewish, a very distinguished gentleman. When Hitler came, the girl left the school. She had different friends, and it-- we were-- it was finished-- finished. And the woman left her husband and went with a very prominent Nazi. I read about them in a book later on. She went-- that was her boyfriend. A horrible man, a horrible man!

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the father?

speaker: interviewee
text: The father-- the girl-- the daughter stayed with her father. And I tried to find them through the Red Cross, and, as they said, if it's not a familial relation, I couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know now-- the only thing, she can be alive. She is my age. The father is-- was also much, much older. And we grew up together. When she had a bath downstairs, I went to her. [INAUDIBLE] she'd be-- you know, we slept together and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith Herman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Herman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Tell me about going to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to different school. First I went to public school for-- till I was 10. And then I went to a private school. I went to a private school, then-- I don't-- I-- I went to two private school. One, maybe I didn't go to attend. Maybe I-- I went from-- from 8 till 10 to one private school, and then from 10 to 12 to another private school. And this second private school was very good. It was a Catholic school. They were very much against Hitler. And I remember when the children started to wear these little pins with the swastika, the headmistress went in-- in the yard and took it off and threw it away. But then when-- later on, one of my teachers wore an SS uniform, SS uniform, into a class. Came in-- and [INAUDIBLE]. And it was a very religious school. The teacher would come into the class, and he would say, 'blessed is the Lord,' and [INAUDIBLE]. The children had to say, 'forever and ever, amen.' And in the corner stood the statue of Maria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they had to ask, what would you rather say, this or 'Heil Hitler'? Would you believe that? And they said, 'Heil Hitler.' I never-- I never put my hand up. I couldn't. But in this school, it was OK, you know? Why did you move schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very good, because the Catholic school was the best. There was-- you can't-- you could only-- they could only have a certain percentage of Jewish people in each school. And I was non-Aryan. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went there because you were Jewish and it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I think I went there before, because we went away so early on from Germany-- '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your childhood at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not very much. Not very much. Very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you considered Jewish by the non-- the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what I was considered in the Catholic school, but I certainly hadn't-- didn't have any religious-- The Catholics were very, very firm, you know? They were very, very.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never experienced any anti-Semitism at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Yes, I experienced once anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know how they taught the Christian children that Jews was-- Jesus was killed by Jews? You know? And that's how this went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the children learned that and they came back from their religious class and went to the Jewish children, and said, you-- you-- you killed Christ. That was the only thing I can remember, the only one thing I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the rise of Nazism in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. You can't-- you couldn't miss it. You could not miss it. And we were always in the [INAUDIBLE], and so they had to stand like that, and things like that. It was horrible. Horrible. And the marching, and the speeches, and this crazy man. And my father was such a special man. He had visions and things like that. He-- we had a wonderful library at home, and amongst it was a-- [INAUDIBLE]-- amongst it was a very big book. And my father was searching something, and he couldn't find it. And he dreamt it was on a certain page and got up, and it was there. And once he came home-- long before Hitler-- he was in the ghetto. There was a ghetto in Breslau. Many Jewish people lived there. And he saw a death writing sword, and he was really shaken. That was long before Hitler. He had a vision. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he was not a frightened man. I remember there was once, we heard like somebody shot somebody. He went and searched. He was never frightened. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family know any people that became members of the Nazi organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father had a-- in Berlin, he was also a partner of a-- of a apothecary. And you had to have a lot of money to-- to open up such an establishment. And also, you can't-- you couldn't be an-- your own apothecary before you were 40. You had to work so-and-so many years as a-- another, because the medications were not already-made. You had to mix them, you know? And this man, my father gives him money, and my uncle helped. And it was a fantastic new, modern, apothecary. And after the war, they said they didn't know us. My mother went to Berlin. They didn't know us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your family talked about when the Nazis started to gain power?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they talk--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they talk about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left-- they must have. A lot-- must have talked a lot about it, because I don't remember. When did he come to power, actually? I-- I-- I went a couple of months later, because my school year was finished, and I lived with my aunt and my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my-- my uncle was a-- was a traveler for-- for printing. He had a printing-- you know this-- this old fashioned printings? And he had these metal plates always with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when Hitler came, you had to give all the gold away. And we had gold in a-- in a-- a curtain rod. And my father exchanged the gold into platinum. They had-- this was-- this was a chemist or something. And then my father spoke to my, because uncle said he'll bring me to Katowice. And on the station he said, I can't bring you-- on the station. I know I had a ski jacket on with two pockets here. And-- but I give you something here into the pockets. You give it, when you arrive there, to your parents. And that was the platinum. Like big chocolate blocks. And now you have to go to this-- to-- into the train, because the luggage was searched. When I put-- did like that, it fell like that. But I did-- I went though. I didn't know what I had. I didn't know. I had the whole platinum with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the effect of the laws against the Jews, the Nuremberg Laws, on your family. [DOOR OPENING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon? Who is that? Mama?

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll have to stop the film.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oma?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. Wait a second. Stop. Can you stop the film?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry for that interruption. Mrs. Picker's 100-year-old mother wandered into the room. I was asking you about the effects of the laws against the Jews on your family. Can you describe those for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was tremendous effects. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For instance, we broke with all people who had anything to do with-- with Nazi. For instance, my auntie, my mother's sister, had a-- the girls got all beautiful houses for-- as a-- from their father. He was a very rich man. And this house-- she was a house owner in a luxury house-- and they were asked to put the German flag up, and they did it. And my father said, we don't put a foot in this house anymore. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was terrible. And my-- my-- my girlfriend downstairs? We lived in that same house, we didn't speak together. It was finished. What about the effect on your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very bad, because the-- the-- it was very bad in Breslau. They put the J all the time on it. And not only that, if they stayed there, they prevented people to go in, to try to. Some people didn't listen to it, but it was bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that affect your family's income?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I am sure it did. Sure. I'm sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father wanted to come to Australia, and he had a dream he would-- he would-- he would grow herbs. That was-- he was-- he wanted to grow herbs, herb medicine things, you know? That was his dream. He wouldn't have it, because he wasn't a farmer and too old. What do you remember? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only know when my girlfriend-- like, we were sisters, you know? And then suddenly, there was no contact. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that-- that's the only thing I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At school I didn't feel anything. I had no-- only that-- that the teacher in the Nazi uniform came, the mathematics teacher. That was a shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it about this time that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he wasn't bad. He wasn't-- he wasn't bad to Jewish children. I must say that. No, no. That-- he didn't do that. They didn't know yet what was coming, I'm sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it about this time that you decided that you would call yourself a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as we came to Poland and I was booked in the school and I was asked my nationality, I thought, I can't say I'm German nationality. How can I say that? I said 'Jewish.' That was it, for always. I was-- I was 12 years old then.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was very hurt, because I saw my father suffering. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He loved Germany. He loved it. All his roots were there, you know? But he was everywhere loved, everywhere. No matter where he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm sure that he missed Germany. He was sure that can't go on longer than six weeks. Absolutely sure. He said, we will be back in six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the decision to leave Germany. How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't know. My-- my-- may parents-- [COUGHS] My Parents must have taken the decision, and didn't-- they didn't ask me or anything. I went. It was-- there was no question or arguing or anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland, to Katowice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stayed there till '39, just before the war. Three days before the war, we fled to-- to the Russian border, because my-- my father said, if anything happens, then the Russians will be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you go to Poland and not leave Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't we didn't know that-- that-- we-- we-- we thought we can go back to Germany in no time. We really did think so. My father thought so. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He thought that can't last, because it was a crazy man. His speeches and all that. It is unbelievable. It's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- it was a bit difficult, because every three months we had to get the new permission to live there. And sometimes we didn't get it. And then we had a home in Czechoslovakia. But everywhere-- it was everywhere very nice. And Czechoslovakia was lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: We always had one room. We all three slept together, you know? It was-- wasn't-- but it wasn't-- that wasn't hardship, really.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where the money came from, but-- I mean, there wasn't social services then. My mother must have had enough money to. And then we had the platinum, and this-- my father maybe earned [INAUDIBLE] in there or something. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were in Poland, your mother set up a business there again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was quite clever doing that, you know? She had fantastic clientele. The top [INAUDIBLE] 10,000 [INAUDIBLE] thousands. It's an-- for instance, a lady would say she is coming, and she-- she wasn't to be interrupted. And she had a chauffeur. And she had-- she had-- servants. They had to wear gloves when serving. Embroidered-- everything embroidered. And hats, things, head gear, and everything. People like that, she had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And money was no obstacle, but-- and there were owners of-- where you dig for coal? Coal? What this it-- what is it called? Mining.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mining, yeah. They had mines, coal mines. So you can image.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father worked whilst you were in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I remember, but maybe he did something. I can't remember. He didn't get a job or anything like that. But

speaker: interviewer
text: He was very clever. When we had such-- such difficulties with-- with staying in Poland, he once, went to the-- went to the police and went to the highest man in the police. What would he be like? Like-- like a commissioner. And he waited till he came out and followed him home, and he knew where he lived. Next day he went and knocked at the door, and his wife was there. He was a gentleman, you know? I mean, nobody could close the door on him. And he introduced himself and told us a story that [INAUDIBLE] was so difficult. Could she help us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we became friends. And through her husband, the life of my mother and my life was bad, later on. She fled with us to-- to-- to Russia, to Katowice. Together we fled with this woman. And we were taken by the police, my mother and I, because my mother had stacks of new money, completely new. They paid her whatever-- whatever what they were owing, and they said we are spies. And they had us in the police station, and they-- they said we are spies. And-- and-- and we said, ask this lady about us. And she said, ring the police station. And-- and-- in fact, the police station was closed, I believe. But he was there just by-- by incident, he was there. And he said, no. These people are OK. And they saved us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- Olsha. His name was Olsha. And she, he name was-- my mother might remember her. Olsha.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop there. That's good.
